affected_groups: rural communities with fragile school viability
content.decision_question: Can a phased structural transition be sequenced reliably enough, given workforce and demographic uncertainty, to avoid a Polish-style capacity shock, or does uncertainty argue for delaying structural reform until capacity conditions stabilize?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence on teacher-training pipeline trends, cohort projections, and comparative phasing outcomes elsewhere can narrow uncertainty about feasibility and risk magnitude. It cannot determine how much implementation risk is tolerable in pursuit of the equity goal, nor whether delay itself constitutes an acceptable cost — those are judgments about risk tolerance, not facts alone.
content.tension: The structural reform proposal explicitly designs multi-cohort phasing to avoid Poland's abrupt 2017 reversal shock, but this sequencing strategy depends on stable-enough demographic projections and adequate teacher-workforce capacity, both of which are themselves in flux (accelerating fertility decline, 16,000-teacher shortfall, aging workforce), creating a compounding uncertainty that phasing alone may not resolve.
content.title: Implementation sequencing risk: phased structural transition versus capacity to execute it
